engineCodes.h3: Compare costs for every major Volkswagen engine — EA189 TDI, EA288 TDI, EA888 TSI and EA211 TSI. All prices are typical UK market ranges for professionally rebuilt engines with warranty. Click "Get Quote" on any engine to receive tailored prices from our network of UK Volkswagen specialists.

groups.name: EA189 2.0 TDI
groups.failureNote: The Dieselgate-era engine family. Known for DPF/EGR clogging and oil pump drive failure. Rebuilt units include revised oil pump drive components. *— Get Quote →* after each row above. Common failure points (expandable note): EA189 engines are known for oil pump drive hex key wear causing oil pressure loss, plus DPF and EGR valve clogging on higher-mileage units. Our rebuilt units include revised oil pump drive components and fully cleaned/refurbished DPF and EGR systems.

groups.name: EA288 2.0 TDI
groups.failureNote: The ULEZ-friendly diesel family. Common rail, AdBlue injected. Generally reliable; AdBlue system issues are the main failure driver. *— Get Quote →* after each row above. Common failure points (expandable note): EA288 engines are known for AdBlue pump failures (£650+ repair) and NOx sensor malfunctions that trigger dashboard warnings. Our rebuilt units include refurbished or new AdBlue system components where required.

groups.name: EA888 1.8/2.0 TSI
groups.failureNote: Volkswagen's primary petrol turbo family. Early Gen 1 & 2 suffer timing chain tensioner failure and carbon buildup; Gen 3 is improved but still needs periodic intake cleaning. *— Get Quote →* after each row above. Common failure points (expandable note): EA888 engines are known for timing chain tensioner failure (Gen 1/2) and intake valve carbon buildup from direct injection. Our rebuilt units include upgraded timing chain tensioners and professionally cleaned cylinder heads.

groups.name: EA188 / PD 2.0 TDI
groups.failureNote: The problematic early 2.0 TDI. Crankshaft, injector, and oil pump issues are common. Most owners choose replacement over repair. *— Get Quote →* after each row above. Common failure points (expandable note): EA188 engines are known for crankshaft failure, injector seal leaks, and oil pump drive failure due to inferior balance shaft materials. Our rebuilt units incorporate upgraded oil pump drive assemblies where available.

groups.name: EA211 1.0/1.4/1.5 TSI
groups.failureNote: Modern small-capacity turbo petrol family. Belt-driven camshaft (not chain). Generally reliable; issues are rare. *— Get Quote →* after each row above. Common failure points (expandable note): EA211 engines are generally robust. Some owners report thermostat housing leaks and EVAP system issues. Our rebuilt units include new gaskets and thermostats as standard.

groups.name: Air-Cooled / Classic Engines
groups.failureNote: Classic VW flat-four engines. Replacement demand remains strong for restoration projects. — Get Quote → for specialist classic engine rebuilders. *— Get Quote →* after each row above. Common failure points (expandable note): Classic air-cooled engines are known for oil leaks, cylinder head warping, and crankcase wear in high-mileage units. Our network includes classic Volkswagen specialists offering fully rebuilt engines with upgraded seals and bearings

commonProblems.h2: Common Volkswagen Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense

problems.h4: Timing Chain Tensioner Failure – Why EA888 Petrol Engines Fail Prematurely
problems.affectedModels: Volkswagen Golf GTI Mk5/Mk6 (2004–2013, EA888 Gen1/2 – codes CCZB, CHHC, CDL), Scirocco (2009–2014), Passat CC (2009–2012), Tiguan I (2009–2015), Eos (2009–2014)
problems.rootCause: The original timing chain tensioner uses a weak ratchet mechanism that gradually loses hydraulic pressure, allowing the chain to slacken and skip teeth. When the chain jumps, valves contact pistons, requiring a full cylinder head rebuild or complete engine replacement. Later Gen3 EA888 engines (2015‑on) use a revised tensioner design but are not immune to long‑term chain stretch.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace timing chain, tensioner, guides, and lower cover (oil pump drive chain also recommended). | 60,000–80,000 miles. Ideal if engine runs fine with no rattles on cold start. Prevents catastrophic failure. |

repairOptions.tier: | Major – Cylinder Head Rebuild (after chain jump) |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Remove head, replace all bent valves, reseat existing valves where salvageable, new head gasket, timing components. | 60,000+ miles if rebuild is done properly.
repairOptions.longevity: Only viable if piston damage is minimal. |

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of professionally rebuilt EA888 engine with upgraded Gen3‑style tensioner, new chains, guides, resealed block, bench tested. | 80,000–100,000+ miles. Best value for high‑mileage engines or when piston/cylinder wall damage has occurred. |

problems.recommendation: On a Volkswagen Golf GTI or Scirocco with a Gen1/Gen2 EA888 engine, if you hear chain rattle on cold start, replace the tensioner and chain immediately – it’s a £800–£1,300 specialist job versus a £4,000+ engine replacement. If the chain has already jumped and the engine still runs poorly, a rebuilt replacement engine is usually cheaper and more reliable than a cylinder head rebuild on a high‑mileage bottom end.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt EA888 engine prices for your Golf GTI or Scirocco

problems.group: EA189 Diesel Oil Pump Drive Failure
problems.h4: EA189 Diesel Oil Pump Drive Failure – The 2.0 TDI Silent Killer
problems.affectedModels: Volkswagen Golf Mk5/Mk6 (2004–2014, engines BKD, CBAB, CFFB, CFGB), Passat B6/B7 (2005–2014), Tiguan I (2008–2015), Touran (2006–2015), Caddy (2007–2015)
problems.rootCause: The oil pump is driven by a hexagon‑shaped key that wears round over time, especially on engines with longer service intervals. When the key strips, oil pressure drops to zero without warning, causing crankshaft and turbo bearing failure. By the time the oil pressure light appears, damage is usually already fatal.

repairOptions.tier: | Preventative – Uprated Oil Pump Drive Kit |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace hex key with revised billet steel drive key, new oil pump or balance shaft assembly (as required), new oil and filter. | 100,000+ miles.
repairOptions.longevity: Only viable if no oil pressure loss has occurred and engine sounds healthy. |

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Engine‑out job. Replace crankshaft, main/rod bearings, oil pump assembly, oil cooler, turbo inspection. | 60,000–80,000 miles. Only worthwhile if the rest of the engine is low‑mileage and body is excellent. |

| Supply & fit of fully rebuilt EA189 engine with billet oil pump drive, new rings, bearings, timing belt, water pump, and 12‑month warranty. | 80,000–120,000 miles. The most cost‑effective solution for engines that have lost oil pressure. |

problems.recommendation: If your EA189 TDI has covered over 80,000 miles and still runs well, fit a billet oil pump drive key preventatively – it costs under £800 at a specialist and protects against total loss. Once the oil pressure light comes on, do not drive the car. Replacing the entire engine with a rebuilt unit (with billet drive included) is almost always cheaper than a crankshaft replacement on a worn bottom end.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt EA189 2.0 TDI engines with uprated oil pump drive

problems.h4: AdBlue & NOx Sensor Failure – EA288 Diesel Electronics
problems.affectedModels: Volkswagen Passat B8 (2015–2019, engines CRLB, CUNA), Golf Mk7 (2013–2020), Tiguan II (2016–2023), Transporter T6 (2016–2019, CXEC), Arteon (2017–2023)
problems.rootCause: The AdBlue injection pump and NOx sensors on EA288 engines are prone to crystallisation and electronic failure. A failed AdBlue pump locks the system, triggering a “No restart in X miles” countdown. NOx sensor failure illuminates the engine light and can force reduced power mode.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace faulty upstream or downstream NOx sensor, clear fault codes, road test. | 40,000–60,000 miles. Straightforward fix if no other damage exists. |
| Replace AdBlue injection pump, flush AdBlue tank, reset SCR counters with diagnostic tool. | 60,000+ miles. Standard repair. |

| Supply & fit rebuilt EA288 engine. Rarely needed for AdBlue issues alone – only when component failure has led to internal damage or car is high‑mileage. | 80,000+ miles. Overkill for electronic faults unless combined with high‑mileage wear. |

problems.recommendation: For a Passat or Golf with AdBlue countdown warnings but otherwise running well, replace the pump or sensor at a specialist – it’s significantly cheaper than a dealer. If you plan to keep the car long‑term and are not concerned about MOT emissions (e.g., for tracked or off‑road vehicles), an AdBlue delete remap can be a permanent solution, though it is not road‑legal. We do not recommend engine replacement for electronic SCR failures.
problems.cta: → Compare EA288 diesel engine prices – or get a quote for AdBlue system repair

problems.h4: EA888 Intake Valve Carbon Buildup – Direct Injection Performance Loss
problems.affectedModels: Volkswagen Golf GTI Mk6/Mk7 (2009–2020, EA888 Gen2/Gen3), Scirocco (2009–2017), Passat B7/B8 (2011–2020), Tiguan I/II (2010–2023)
problems.rootCause: Direct injection petrol engines do not spray fuel over the intake valves, so oil vapour from the crankcase ventilation system bakes onto the valves over time. Thick carbon deposits restrict airflow, causing cold start misfires, reduced power, and higher fuel consumption.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Remove intake manifold, media‑blast valves with crushed walnut shells, clean ports, reassemble with new gaskets. | 30,000–50,000 miles. Highly effective. The standard specialist solution. |
| Inject chemical cleaner through intake or hydrogen‑carbon cleaning system. | 15,000–20,000 miles. Less effective on heavy deposits but good for maintenance. |
| Only required if carbon buildup has caused valve sticking or burnt valve seats. | Not recommended for carbon alone – walnut blast first. |

problems.recommendation: Carbon buildup is inevitable on all direct‑injection EA888 engines. A walnut blast at a specialist costs £400–£650 every 40,000–60,000 miles and restores full performance. Do not pay dealer prices for the same service. Chemical cleaning is cheaper but less thorough – fine for maintenance between blasts. This problem alone does not justify engine replacement.
problems.cta: → Find a Volkswagen specialist for EA888 walnut blasting – or compare rebuilt engine prices if yours has other issues

problems.h4: Crankshaft & Bearing Failure – EA188 / PD 2.0 TDI Weak Bottom End
problems.affectedModels: Volkswagen Golf Mk5 (2004–2008, engines AZV, BMM, BMN), Passat B6 (2005–2008), Touran (2004–2010), Caddy (2004–2010)
problems.rootCause: The early 2.0 TDI PD (Pumpe‑Düse) engines use a forged crankshaft with inadequate bearing clearances and poor oil supply at idle. Combined with the same hex key oil pump drive as EA189, many suffer spun main bearings or complete crankshaft seizure. Once the crank has spun a bearing, the engine is scrap.

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – Preventative Oil Pump Drive Upgrade |
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replace hex drive with billet key, new oil pump or balance shaft unit, fresh oil. | 80,000+ miles. Only if the engine is quiet and has no bearing knock. |

| Engine‑out. Remove crank, regrind journals, fit oversize bearings, replace oil pump, rebuild bottom end. | 60,000–80,000 miles. Viable only if the crank is salvageable (no deep scoring). Labour‑intensive. |

| Supply & fit fully rebuilt EA188 engine with billet oil pump drive, new bearings, piston rings, head gasket, timing belt. | 80,000+ miles. Almost always the better choice once bearing damage is confirmed. |

problems.recommendation: If your EA188 2.0 TDI has a deep knocking noise that worsens with revs, stop driving immediately. A rebuilt replacement engine costs £2,200–£3,500 supplied and fitted – often less than a crankshaft regrind on a 150,000‑mile block. If caught absolutely early (a faint knock only at idle), a specialist may fit new bearings in situ, but this is rare. For most owners, a rebuilt unit is the definitive fix.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt EA188 2.0 TDI engine prices for your Golf or Passat

engineTypes.h2: Volkswagen Engine Types – Options, Costs & What to Expect
engineTypes.intro: Every Volkswagen engine type available through our UK network, with typical price ranges and recommendations based on your vehicle’s age, condition and your budget.

types.title: Volkswagen Reconditioned Engines (Recon)
types.description: A stripped, cleaned and fully inspected engine where all worn components are replaced with OEM‑spec parts. Best for owners who want factory reliability without the factory price. For EA189 and EA288 diesel engines, reconditioning includes a revised oil pump drive to prevent future failure.
types.priceRange: Typical price range: £1,600 – £3,800 (depending on engine code)

types.title: Volkswagen Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A complete rebuild with upgraded components addressing known failure points – such as reinforced timing chain tensioners (EA888 TSI) and billet oil pump drive keys (2.0 TDI) – for a longer service life than a standard replacement.

types.title: Used Volkswagen Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out unit from a donor vehicle. The most affordable option when budget is the priority, with verified history and warranty available.

types.title: Volkswagen Remanufactured Engines
types.description: A factory‑spec, as‑new engine: every surface is machined, every component is replaced, and the unit is built to meet exact OEM tolerances. The premium choice for performance models like Golf R or Touareg V6 TDI, or owners wanting a zero‑mile engine.

types.title: Volkswagen Refurbished Engines
types.description: A lightly serviced, cleaned, and tested engine that works reliably but hasn’t had a complete rebuild. Often a pragmatic, fast solution for older or budget‑sensitive models like a high‑mileage Passat or Touran where a full recon isn’t economical.

types.title: Volkswagen Supply & Fit Engines
types.description: Your replacement engine delivered and professionally installed by a UK Volkswagen specialist. Includes removal of the old unit, fitting, fluids, and a workmanship warranty. Strongly recommended for EA888 chain replacements and Transporter engine swaps.
types.priceRange: Typical added cost: £450 – £800 on top of engine price
types.cta: Find Volkswagen engine supply & fit deals

engineTypes.closing: All engine types include a minimum 12‑month warranty. Prices are typical UK market ranges and depend on exact engine code, supplier, and any additional fitting services. Use our free comparison tool to get a tailored quote for your Volkswagen model today.

engineSizes.h2: Volkswagen Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model

items.title: Volkswagen 1.2L Diesel Engines
items.description: The smallest Volkswagen diesel, this 3-cylinder TDI unit was used mainly in the Polo and small-platform models. It offered excellent economy but suffers from turbocharger wear and DPF clogging on short journeys
items.cta: Get quotes for Volkswagen 1.2L diesel engines

items.title: Volkswagen 1.4L Diesel Engines
items.description: A 3-cylinder TDI used across small Volkswagens before the brand standardised on 1.6L units. Known for good fuel economy but limited power output
items.cta: Get quotes for Volkswagen 1.4L diesel engines

items.title: Volkswagen 1.6L Diesel Engines
items.description: A hugely popular 4-cylinder TDI found in nearly every medium Volkswagen model. Covers both the older PD (Pumpe-Düse) and later common rail EA189 family. Replacement demand remains very high
items.cta: Get quotes for Volkswagen 1.6L diesel engines
commonFailurePoints: oil pump drive hex key wear (EA189)

items.title: Volkswagen 1.9L Diesel Engines
items.description: The legendary SDI and TDI PD unit. Renowned for longevity, but mounting emissions regulations and high mileage make replacement common on older classics like the Bora and Mk4 Golf
items.cta: Get quotes for Volkswagen 1.9L diesel engines

items.title: Volkswagen 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The backbone of Volkswagen’s diesel range for 20 years. Includes the problematic EA188 PD unit, the massive-volume EA189 common rail (Dieselgate), and the later EA288. Thousands replaced annually in the UK
items.cta: Get quotes for Volkswagen 2.0L diesel engines

items.title: Volkswagen 2.5L Diesel Engines
items.description: A 5-cylinder TDI used in the Transporter T5 and early Touareg. Known for torque and character but discontinued in favour of 2.0 BiTDI units. Replacement units are sought after by van owners
items.cta: Get quotes for Volkswagen 2.5L diesel engines

items.title: Volkswagen 3.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The V6 TDI, shared with Audi, used in the large SUVs. A relatively robust unit compared to smaller diesels, but expensive to rebuild
items.cta: Get quotes for Volkswagen 3.0L diesel engines

items.title: Volkswagen 1.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The modern EA211 3-cylinder TSI found in the Up, Polo and small crossovers. Direct injection, turbocharged and efficient. Reliability is generally good; failures are often electrical
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen 1.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volkswagen 1.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: A 3- or 4-cylinder unit bridging older MPi and newer TSI technology. The early 1.2 TSI had timing chain issues; later belts were more reliable
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen 1.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volkswagen 1.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: A complex family: early chain-driven 16v units, twin-charger TSI (GTI), later EA211 belt-driven TSI. Replacement demand is high on twin-charger units due to supercharger clutch failure
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen 1.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volkswagen 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The EA211 Evo TSI, designed to replace the 1.4 TSI. Features variable geometry turbo and cylinder deactivation. Early examples had vibration/judder issues
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen 1.5L petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: judder at low RPM (software fix available)

items.title: Volkswagen 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: An older 8v and 16v unit found in the Golf Mk4 and Bora era. Simple to work on, but high-mileage units suffer oil consumption. Replaced by 1.4/1.5 TSI in newer models
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volkswagen 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: A core performance and family-car engine for decades. Includes the non-turbo 1.8 (Golf Mk4) and the famous 1.8T 20v (Audi/SEAT/Škoda family), plus later EA888 Gen1. Timing belt or chain dependent
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen 1.8L petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: EA888 Gen1 – timing chain tensioner failure

items.title: Volkswagen 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: Volkswagen’s most dominant petrol engine of the 21st century. EA888 Gen1/2/3/4 powers everything from the Golf GTI to the Tiguan and Arteon. Replacement demand driven by timing chain and oil consumption issues on earlier generations
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volkswagen 2.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: A 5-cylinder unit (Volvo-sourced design) used briefly in the US-market Jetta and Beetle. Very rare in the UK but present on imported models
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen 2.5L petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: Limited UK data – vacuum pump oil leaks

items.title: Volkswagen 3.2L & 3.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The VR6 range. Narrow-angle V6 that fits in Golf-sized bays. Used in the R32 (3.2) and Passat R36 (3.6). Enthusiast favourites with high replacement costs
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen VR6 petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: high oil consumption on higher-mileage units

items.title: Volkswagen Plug-In Hybrid (GTE & eHybrid) Engines
items.description: Volkswagen’s PHEV system pairs a 1.4 TSI with an electric motor. Shared across VW Group (Golf GTE, Passat GTE). Battery degradation and electrical faults are the primary replacement drivers. System code(s): CUKB, DQ400e gearbox-integrated motor
items.cta: Enquire about Volkswagen hybrid engine availability

items.title: Volkswagen Electric Motors (BEV)
items.description: The MEB platform permanent-magnet synchronous motor used on ID models. Failures are rare, but controllers and bearings can fail. Motor code(s) APP 310 (APP310) – ID.3, ID.4, ID.5; APP 550 (ID.7)

engineCodeDirectory.h2: Volkswagen Engine Codes – Most Replaced Engines (2008–2025)
engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Volkswagen engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Volkswagen specialists.

entries.title: BKD 2.0 TDI
entries.description: The BKD was Volkswagen’s first 2.0 16v common‑rail diesel, fitted to the Golf Mk5, Passat B6, and Audi A3 (8P) from 2003‑2008. The engine is otherwise robust, but the oil pump drive hex key wears round over time, leading to sudden oil pressure loss and crankshaft bearing failure. A quality rebuilt BKD includes a billet steel oil pump drive key and new bearings.
entries.cta: Get quotes for BKD Volkswagen engine replacement

entries.title: CBAB 2.0 TDI
entries.description: Found in the Golf Mk6, Passat B7, and Tiguan I from 2008‑2013, the CBAB is a refinement of the EA189 family. It shares the same oil pump hex key vulnerability as the BKD, plus DPF clogging on short‑journey cars. Rebuilt units come with a revised oil pump drive and a fully cleaned or replaced DPF.
entries.cta: Compare CBAB Volkswagen engine prices

entries.title: CFFB 2.0 TDI
entries.description: Fitted to the Passat B7, CC, and Tiguan I from 2009‑2014. CFFB engines are known for EGR cooler cracking (coolant loss) and the same oil pump drive failure as other EA189s. Rebuilt engines include an uprated billet oil pump drive and a refurbished EGR cooler.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CFFB Volkswagen engine replacement

entries.title: CFGB 2.0 TDI 170bhp
entries.description: The higher‑output 170bhp version of the EA189, used in the Golf Mk6 GTD, Passat B7, and Scirocco (2009‑2014). In addition to the standard EA189 oil pump issue, CFGB engines suffer from turbocharger vane sticking and injector seal leaks. Rebuilt units include a billet oil pump drive, turbo refurbishment, and new injector seals.
entries.cta: Compare CFGB Volkswagen engine prices

entries.title: CAYC 1.6 TDI
entries.description: The 1.6‑litre EA189 diesel, fitted to the Golf Mk6, Polo Mk5, Passat B7, and Caddy from 2009‑2015. Extremely sensitive to DPF clogging (short journeys) and EGR valve failure. The 1.6 also shares the oil pump drive weakness of the 2.0‑litre units. Rebuilt CAYC engines include a billet drive, cleaned DPF, and a refurbished EGR valve.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CAYC Volkswagen engine replacement

entries.title: CRLB 2.0 TDI 150bhp
entries.description: The Euro‑6 EA288 diesel, fitted to the Passat B8 (2015‑2019), Tiguan II, and Škoda Superb. AdBlue (SCR) system failures dominate: AdBlue pump crystallisation and NOx sensor failure trigger countdown‑to‑no‑start warnings. Mechanical failures are rare. Rebuilt CRLB engines include a refurbished AdBlue system where required.
entries.cta: Compare CRLB Volkswagen engine prices

entries.title: CUNA 2.0 TDI 150bhp
entries.description: Used in the Golf Mk7, Passat B8, and Tiguan II from 2015‑2020. Same EA288 platform as CRLB. AdBlue injector crystallisation and EGR cooler leaks are the primary failure drivers. Rebuilt units include cleaned or replaced AdBlue components.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CUNA Volkswagen engine replacement

entries.title: CUPA 2.0 TDI 184bhp
entries.description: The higher‑output EA288, found in the Golf Mk7 GTD (2015‑2020) and Passat B8. Shares the same AdBlue/SCR weaknesses as the 150bhp units. Some owners report DPF backpressure sensor failure. Rebuilt CUPA engines include a DPF clean and AdBlue system health check.
entries.cta: Compare CUPA Volkswagen engine prices

entries.title: CXEC 2.0 TDI (Transporter T6)
entries.description: Fitted to the Transporter T6, Caravelle, and California from 2016‑2019. The EA288 van‑spec engine is tuned for low‑end torque. AdBlue pump failure is the most common replacement driver, along with EGR cooler leaks. Rebuilt CXEC engines include a refurbished AdBlue system and EGR cooler.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CXEC Volkswagen engine replacement

entries.title: CCZB 2.0 TSI (EA888 Gen2)
entries.description: Found in the Golf GTI Mk6, Scirocco, and Passat CC from 2009‑2013. The Gen2 EA888 is notorious for timing chain tensioner failure – the tensioner ratchet fails, chain jumps, and valves hit pistons. Rebuilt CCZB engines include the revised Gen3‑style tensioner, new chains, and a decarbonised cylinder head.
entries.cta: Compare CCZB Volkswagen engine prices

entries.title: CDL 2.0 TSI (EA888 Gen2 – Golf R)
entries.description: The 265bhp Golf R Mk6 engine (2010‑2013). Same timing chain tensioner weakness as the standard CCZB, but with a larger turbo and stronger internals. Replacement demand is high due to tuning‑related failures and tensioner collapse. Rebuilt CDL engines include uprated chains, revised tensioner, and performance bearings.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CDL Volkswagen engine replacement

entries.title: CHHC 2.0 TSI (EA888 Gen2)
entries.description: Fitted to the Golf GTI Mk6, Scirocco, and Eos from 2009‑2012. Same timing chain tensioner failure as other Gen2 EA888s. Also known for intake manifold runner flap failure (P2015 code). Rebuilt CHHC units include revised tensioner, new manifold flaps, and head decarbonisation.
entries.cta: Compare CHHC Volkswagen engine prices

entries.title: DNUE 2.0 TSI (EA888 Gen3)
entries.description: The Gen3 EA888, fitted to the Golf GTI Mk7.5 (2017‑2020), Tiguan II, and Arteon. Chain tensioner failures are rare, but water pump / thermostat housing leaks are common, along with intake valve carbon buildup. Rebuilt DNUE engines include a new water pump assembly and professionally cleaned cylinder head.
entries.cta: Get quotes for DNUE Volkswagen engine replacement

entries.title: DKZA 2.0 TSI (EA888 Gen4)
entries.description: The latest EA888 generation, found in the Golf GTI Mk8, Golf R Mk8, and Tiguan R from 2020‑present. Early examples show oil consumption and PCV system failures. Rebuilt DKZA units include upgraded PCV valves and revised piston rings.
entries.cta: Compare DKZA Volkswagen engine prices

entries.title: BKP 2.0 TDI PD
entries.description: Fitted to the Passat B6 and Audi A3 from 2005‑2008. The PD (unit injector) system is robust, but crankshaft bearing wear (due to poor oil supply) and injector seal leaks (diesel in oil) are common. Rebuilt BKP engines include new bearings, revised oil pump assembly, and new injector seals.
entries.cta: Get quotes for BKP Volkswagen engine replacement

entries.title: BMM 2.0 TDI PD
entries.description: Used in the Touran, Caddy, and Eos from 2006‑2010. Same PD weaknesses as BKP: injector seal leaks cause oil dilution and potential engine runaway. Dual mass flywheel (DMF) failure is also common. Rebuilt BMM engines include new injector seals, bearings, and a DMF recommendation.
entries.cta: Compare BMM Volkswagen engine prices

entries.title: AZV 2.0 TDI PD
entries.description: One of the earliest 2.0 PD engines, fitted to the Golf Mk5 and Touran from 2003‑2006. Known for crankshaft failure (sudden snapping) and oil pump drive issues. Rebuilt AZV units include a billet oil pump drive and a crack‑tested crankshaft.
entries.cta: Get quotes for AZV Volkswagen engine replacement

entries.title: CAVE / CTHE 1.4 TSI Twincharger
entries.description: The infamous twin‑charged (supercharger + turbo) 1.4 TSI, fitted to the Polo GTI Mk5 (2010‑2014) and Golf Mk6. Failures include supercharger clutch sticking, timing chain stretch, and piston ring failure (oil burning). Rebuilt units include revised timing chains, supercharger clutch repair, and new piston rings.
entries.cta: Compare CAVE & CTHE Volkswagen engine prices

entries.title: CBZB 1.2 TSI
entries.description: Found in the Polo Mk5, Golf Mk6, and Passat B7 from 2010‑2015. Early CBZB engines had timing chain stretch (chain jumps, engine interference). Rebuilt CBZB engines include the revised timing chain kit and tensioner.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CBZB Volkswagen engine replacement

entries.title: CHYA 1.0 MPI / TSI
entries.description: The EA211 1.0 3‑cylinder, fitted to the Up, Polo Mk6, and T‑Cross from 2011‑present. Generally reliable, but thermostat housing leaks and ignition coil failures occur. Rebuilt CHYA units include a new water pump housing and fresh coils.
entries.cta: Compare CHYA Volkswagen engine prices

entries.title: BFS / BFS 1.6 FSI
entries.description: The early direct‑injection petrol (FSI), fitted to the Golf Mk5 and Passat B6 from 2003‑2008. Intake valve carbon buildup and fuel pump (high‑pressure) failure are common. Rebuilt FSI engines include a decarbonised head and a refurbished HPFP. Closing line (Block A): Can’t find your engine code listed above? engineYears.h2: Volkswagen Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When

years.preview: 1.9 TDI PD Debut, EA113 2.0 8v & VR6 Peak Era Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The late 1990s saw Volkswagen introduce two of the most enduring engines in the brand's UK history - the EA113 1.8 20v turbocharged petrol across Golf Mk4 and Passat B5, and the revolutionary Pumpe-Düse (PD) unit-injector diesel that would redefine diesel performance expectations for an entire decade.
keyChanges: Volkswagen Golf Mk4 (1997) launches with EA113 1.8 20v turbocharged petrol (AGU/ARZ/AUM/AWU/BAM), 2.0 8v (APK/AQY), and the 1.9 TDI in both SDI and TDI guise
Pumpe-Düse (PD) unit-injector 1.9 TDI debuts from 1999–2000 - high-pressure fuel delivery via cam-driven individual injectors, replacing common-rail on volume Golf/Passat applications
Passat B5 (1997) adopts same EA113 petrol family longitudinally; 2.3 V5 and 2.8 VR6 4Motion introduced as volume range-toppers
Golf R32 (2002) introduces the 3.2 VR6 24v (BFH/BML) - 241hp narrowangle six-cylinder, flagship performance engine
Euro 3 drives three-way catalyst fitment and revised EGR on all diesel models from 2000
mainEngines: AGU / AUM / ARZ / BAM - 1.8 20v Turbo EA113 - 125/150/180hp petrol (Golf Mk4, Passat B5, Bora)
APK / AQY / AZJ - 2.0 8v EA113 - naturally aspirated petrol (Golf Mk4 base, Bora)
ALH / AGR / ASV - 1.9 TDI (non-PD) - 90/110hp diesel (Golf Mk4, Passat B5 early, Bora)
ATD / AXR / BJB - 1.9 TDI PD - 100/101hp diesel (Golf Mk4, Polo, Lupo)
AHH / ASZ / ATJ - 1.9 TDI PD - 110/130hp (Golf Mk4, Passat B5)
BFH / BML - 3.2 VR6 - 241hp naturally aspirated (Golf R32)
knownFor: 1.9 TDI PD universally regarded as one of the finest diesel engines ever produced - stout cast-iron block, mechanical durability to 300,000+ miles with correct servicing; camshaft lobe wear on high-mileage examples above 200,000 miles the main watch-point

EA113 1.8 20v Turbo in Golf Mk4 praised for strong mid-range torque and linear power delivery - timing belt failure is catastrophic, so strict adherence to the 60,000-mile/5-year interval is essential
Golf R32's 3.2 VR6 provides an extraordinary engine character - linear power delivery, narrow-angle soundtrack, collectible; oil consumption on older examples and coil pack wear are known
2.0 8v is mechanically bullet-proof but uninspiring - widely preferred by buyers wanting longevity with minimum maintenance cost

years.preview: Golf Mk5, FSI Direct Injection & 2.0 TDI EA188 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The launch of the Golf Mk5 in 2003 introduced FSI (Fuel Stratified Injection) direct-injection petrol to the mainstream VW range - a genuine engineering advance - while the new 2.0 TDI EA188 common-rail diesel replaced the PD across the Passat and Golf, and the Touareg delivered VW's first UK SUV with V6 TDI and V8 petrol options.
keyChanges: Golf Mk5 (2003 UK) launches with EA113 1.6 FSI (BAG/BLP) and 2.0 FSI direct-injection petrols - the brand's first mainstream direct-injection petrol in the UK
1.4 16v petrol (BUD/BCA) continues as Golf and Polo entry petrol; 1.6 8v (BSF/BGU) as the NA workhorse
2.0 TDI EA188 (PD variant - BKD/AZV/BMN) introduced on Golf Mk5 and Passat B6 - 140hp PD diesel; refined over previous 1.9 but bigger displacement
Passat B6 (2005) adopts 2.0 TDI PD (BKD) and 2.0 FSI/TFSI as volume engines; 3.2 V6 FSI as top petrol
Touareg (2002) arrives with 2.5 TDI R5 (BAC/BPE) and 3.2 V6 petrol - first VW UK SUV
Euro 3 continuing; early Euro 4 transition from 2005
mainEngines: BAG / BLP - 1.6 FSI - direct-injection petrol (Golf Mk5, Touran)
BLR / BLY / BVY - 2.0 FSI - direct-injection petrol (Golf Mk5, Passat B6)
AXW / BLX / BWA - 2.0 TFSI - turbocharged direct petrol 200hp (Golf GTI Mk5, Passat)
BKD / AZV - 2.0 TDI PD - 140hp (Golf Mk5, Passat B6, Touran)
BMN / CBAB - 2.0 TDI PD - 170hp (Golf GT TDI, Passat)
BJB / BKC / BXE - 1.9 TDI PD - 105hp (Golf Mk5, Polo, Touran)
AXB / AXD - 2.5 TDI R5 - five-cylinder diesel (Touareg)
knownFor: Golf GTI Mk5 with 2.0 TFSI (BYD/AXX) earns enormous UK enthusiasm - strong, tuneable, communicative; early cam follower wear on HPFP drive lobe a documented issue from 60,000 miles
2.0 TDI PD BKD in Golf Mk5 is a robust unit - strong reputation, 200,000+ mile examples common; cam follower wear on unit injector lobes the principal concern if oil changes are missed
1.6 FSI direct-injection suffers intake valve carbon build-up from 50,000 miles due to absence of port injection; walnut blasting increasingly standard on used examples
Passat B6 2.0 TDI widely regarded as one of VW's most competent touring diesel pairings - chassis, engine, and economy combine well; timing belt critical at 4-year/60,000-mile intervals

years.preview: EA888 TSI Launch, EA189 2.0 TDI Common Rail & Timing Chain Concerns Major Engine Family Introduction Major Reliability Revision
years.description: The EA888 1.8/2.0 TSI family launched in 2007–2008 across Golf Mk6, Passat B7 and Tiguan represented VW's biggest petrol engine departure in a decade - a chain-driven, direct-injection turbocharged four-cylinder - while the new common-rail EA189 2.0 TDI replaced the PD diesel across the full range; both families carried reliability issues that the brand spent years correcting.
keyChanges: EA888 Gen 1 1.8 TSI (BYT/BZB/CDAA/CDAB) introduced 2007 - 160hp turbocharged direct-injection petrol, timing chain driven
EA888 Gen 1 2.0 TSI (CCTA/CBFA) introduced 2008 - 200hp, shared chain architecture; timing chain tensioner hydraulic failure documented from early production
EA189 2.0 TDI common-rail (CFGB/CBAB/CFHC) replaces PD diesel across Golf Mk6, Passat B7, Tiguan and Touran
EA111 1.4 TSI with twin-charged technology (TSI + supercharger at low rpm, turbo at high rpm - CAXA/CMBA) introduced on Golf Mk6
Polo Mk5 (2009) arrives with EA111 1.2 TSI (CBZB/CBZC) and 1.4 TDI (BNM/BMS)
Euro 5 compliance from 2010 - DPF standard on all TDI diesel models
mainEngines: CDAA / CDAB / BYT - 1.8 TSI EA888 Gen 1 - 160hp turbopetrol (Golf Mk6, Passat B7, Tiguan, CC)
CCTA / CBFA / CCZB - 2.0 TSI EA888 Gen 1 - 200/210hp turbopetrol (Golf GTI Mk6, Scirocco, Passat)
CBAB / CFGB / CFHC - 2.0 TDI EA189 - 110/140hp common-rail diesel (Golf Mk6, Passat B7, Tiguan, Touran)
CLJA / CLCA / CLCB - 2.0 TDI EA189 - 170hp (Passat B7, CC TDI)
CAXA / CMBA - 1.4 TSI EA111 twin-charged - 122/160hp (Golf Mk6, Scirocco, Touran)
CBZB / CBZC - 1.2 TSI EA111 - 85/105hp (Polo Mk5, Golf Mk6 entry)
CGPA / CHPA - 3.6 VR6 - 300hp (Phaeton, Touareg)
knownFor: EA888 Gen 1 2.0 TSI (CCTA/CBFA) timing chain tensioner failure is the most significant petrol engine reliability event in VW UK history - plastic tensioner collar failure allows chain to skip from as low as 30,000 miles; updated tensioner (06K-109-467) is the mandatory fix; pre-2013 build engines should be checked or replaced proactively
1.8 TSI Gen 1 shares the chain risk but to a lesser degree - oil consumption via piston rings also documented on high-mileage examples
EA189 2.0 TDI common-rail a worthy successor to PD - quieter, more refined, Euro 5 capable, but would later become the epicentre of the Dieselgate emissions scandal
1.4 TSI twin-charged in Golf Mk6 is an engineering showpiece - combined supercharger and turbo seamless power delivery; plastic coolant pipes and water pump are the known weak points at 80,000+ miles

years.preview: Golf Mk7, EA888 Gen 3 & Dieselgate EA189 Scandal Era Major Reliability Revision
years.description: The Golf Mk7's arrival in 2013 brought the EA888 Gen 3 with a substantially improved timing chain tensioner and the EA211 engine family replacing the EA111 across the entry range - but these same years saw EA189 TDI production at its peak, and September 2015 exposed the Dieselgate emissions defeat device scandal affecting 1.2 million VW Group vehicles in the UK alone.
keyChanges: Golf Mk7 (2013) launches with EA888 Gen 3 1.8 TSI (CJSA/CJSB) and 2.0 TSI (CHHB), and EA211 1.2/1.4 TSI (CJZA/CJZB/CZCA/CZDA)
EA888 Gen 3 features substantially revised timing chain tensioner and dual-injection (combined port and direct) for lower carbon build-up - a significant reliability improvement over Gen 1/2
EA211 family (2013) replaces EA111 across Polo, Golf base and Passat - new architecture, lighter, Euro 6-ready
EA189 2.0 TDI (CLCB/DFCA/CRBC) continues as volume diesel across Golf Mk7, Passat B8, Tiguan
September 2015: VW EA189 Dieselgate scandal breaks - 1.2 million UK vehicles recalled for defeat device NOx software; 508,276 VW passenger cars affected
Euro 6 compliance from September 2015 - all new VW diesel models must carry SCR AdBlue or GPF

EA189 2.0 TDI post-recall (software update January 2016 onwards) is a driveable unit but some UK owners report subtle fuelling and DPF behaviour changes post-fix - always verify recall completion status when buying used
Golf GTI Mk7 with EA888 Gen 3 2.0 TSI earns universal praise - definitive hot hatch balance, strong long-term ownership record compared to EA888 Gen 1
Golf R Mk7 300hp 2.0 TSI is the peak expression of this engine generation in the VW range - exploitable, track-capable, well-cooled; highly sought in the UK enthusiast market

years.preview: EA288 Euro 6 TDI, 1.5 TSI Evo & Golf Mk7.5 Major Engine Family Introduction Technology Introduction
years.description: The post-Dieselgate period saw Volkswagen introduce the EA288 2.0 TDI as its fully compliant Euro 6 successor diesel - clean-sheet SCR AdBlue architecture - while the 1.5 TSI Evo EA211 Evo arrived in 2017 with Miller-cycle combustion and cylinder deactivation, replacing the 1.4 TSI as the volume petrol across Golf, Tiguan and Passat.
keyChanges: EA288 2.0 TDI (DFHA/DFCA Euro 6) introduced - full SCR AdBlue architecture, new combustion geometry; Euro 6d-TEMP compliant from 2018
1.5 TSI Evo (DADA/DPCA) launches 2017 on Golf Mk7.5 - 4-cylinder, Miller cycle, cylinder deactivation ACT, 130/150hp; replaces 1.4 TSI as mainstream petrol
1.0 TSI Evo (EA211 three-cylinder, CHZC/DKRA) introduced on Polo Mk6 (2017) and T-Roc (2018) - replaces 1.2 TSI
Golf Mk7.5 facelift (2017) acts as launchpad for EA211 Evo family across the range
T-Roc (2018) and new-generation Tiguan (2016) adopt updated EA288 TDI and EA211 Evo petrols
48V mild-hybrid integration confirmed for 2019 models - GTE PHEV Golf continues with 1.4 TSI + e-motor

1.0 TSI Evo three-cylinder earns positive reception in Polo and T-Roc - refined NVH for its displacement, economical, no major structural concerns flagged in early mileage data
Golf GTE PHEV 1.4 TSI combustion unit sees modest replacement demand independently; hybrid battery health the more critical ownership concern at higher mileage

years.preview: Golf Mk8, ID.3 BEV Launch & 48V MHEV Integration Major Engine Family Introduction Technology Introduction
years.description: The Golf Mk8 (2020) and the ID.3 (2020) arrived in the same UK model year - one the most technically complex Golf in the model's history with embedded 48V mild-hybrid, PHEV and GTE options, and the other VW's first mass-market purpose-built BEV on the MEB electric platform, signalling the formal start of VW's electric era.
keyChanges: Golf Mk8 (2020 UK) launches with 1.0 TSI Evo, 1.5 TSI Evo 48V eTSI mild-hybrid, and 2.0 TDI - new evo2 calibrations for Euro 6d final
1.5 TSI eTSI 48V mild-hybrid (DADA/DPBA) integrates belt-starter-generator on Golf Mk8 - fuel savings of 10–15% in mixed use
ID.3 (2020) launches on MEB platform - 45kWh, 58kWh and 77kWh battery options; APP310 rear e-motor 150–204kW
ID.3 GTX (2023) previewed - dual motor AWD architecture
Golf R Mk8 uses EA888 Gen 3b 2.0 TSI (DKZ/DGE) - 320hp, revised internal engine with stronger internals vs Gen 3
Golf GTE (Mk8) 1.4 TSI eHybrid PHEV - 245hp combined output; 60km EV range

faq.h2: Volkswagen Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Volkswagen owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Volkswagen engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A rebuilt Volkswagen engine typically costs between £1,500 and £4,500 for common models, depending on the engine code. A 1.9 TDI (ALH, BLS) often falls around £1,500–£2,800, while a 2.0 TDI EA189 (BKD, CBAB) runs £1,800–£3,300. A 2.0 TSI EA888 for a Golf GTI usually costs £2,200–£4,200, and a Transporter 2.0 BiTDI (CFCA) can reach £5,500. Fitting adds another £450–£800. Use our marketplace to compare quotes tailored to your exact Volkswagen model.
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen engine prices now

items.question: What affects the cost of a Volkswagen engine replacement?
items.answer: Four key factors drive the price: (1) engine size and family – a 1.2 TSI (CBZB) is cheaper than a 3.0 V6 TDI; (2) engine condition – used is cheapest, rebuilt with upgraded components costs more but lasts longer; (3) vehicle complexity – a Transporter engine swap costs more labour than a Golf; (4) supply-only versus supply-and-fit. Location matters too – labour in London can be 20–30% higher. Our marketplace brings you transparent, fixed-price quotes from UK Volkswagen specialists so there are no surprises.
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen engine prices now

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Volkswagen?
items.answer: In most cases, yes. If your Volkswagen’s chassis, gearbox, and bodywork are solid, a rebuilt engine often costs less than buying another used car of the same vintage – especially for Golf, Passat, and Transporter models that hold value well. A rebuilt engine with upgraded components (billet oil pump drive on EA189, revised chain tensioner on EA888) often outlasts the original. The only time replacement doesn’t make sense is if the car has catastrophic structural rust or a failed gearbox. Use our free tool to compare quotes and decide with real numbers.
items.cta: Get a Volkswagen engine quote now

items.question: What's included in a Volkswagen engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: Supply-and-fit includes: sourcing the correct rebuilt or reconditioned engine for your exact Volkswagen model and engine code, removing the old unit, installing the replacement, all new fluids and filters, a workmanship warranty (typically 12 months), and disposal of the old engine. Most specialists also include a road test after installation. Fitting costs add £450–£800 depending on the model – Transporter and Touareg take more labour than Polo or Golf. Always ask whether the quote includes VAT and delivery of the replacement engine.
items.cta: Get a Volkswagen engine quote now

items.question: How long does a Volkswagen engine replacement take?
items.answer: For common Volkswagen models like the Golf 2.0 TDI or Polo 1.2 TSI, a like-for-like engine swap typically takes 3–5 working days. More complex models – Transporter T5/T6 (engine-out through the cab), Touareg V6 TDI, or any 4Motion variant – can take 5–7 days. The biggest variable is sourcing the correct engine code. Our network of UK specialist suppliers stocks commonly replaced codes (BKD, CBAB, CRLB, CCZB, CAYC, CFCA), which dramatically reduces lead times. Ask your chosen supplier for their current turnaround when you get your quote.
items.cta: Get a Volkswagen engine quote now

items.question: Do reconditioned Volkswagen engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes. Every reconditioned or rebuilt Volkswagen engine supplied through our network comes with a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Many suppliers offer extended 24‑month coverage on remanufactured units and EA888 Gen3/EA288 diesels. The warranty typically covers all major internal components: crankshaft, pistons, conrods, timing chain/belt, oil pump, cylinder head, and gaskets. Labour for fitting-related faults is often included, but always confirm exact terms with your chosen specialist before proceeding. We recommend asking for the warranty document in writing.
items.cta: Find Volkswagen engines with warranty

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Volkswagen engine?
items.answer: Used engines are cheaper upfront – typically £800–£2,200 – and are a good temporary fix if you plan to sell the car soon. However, you inherit unknown mileage and the original failure points (e.g., old timing chains on EA888). Reconditioned engines cost more (£1,800–£4,500) but include upgraded components – billet oil pump drives on 2.0 TDI EA189, revised tensioners on EA888, new rings and bearings throughout. For a Volkswagen you plan to keep for another 3‑5 years, a reconditioned engine with a 12‑24 month warranty is almost always the better investment.

items.question: What are the most common Volkswagen engine failures?
items.answer: The most common replacement-driving failures across Volkswagen engines are: (1) EA888 Gen1/2 timing chain tensioner failure (Golf GTI Mk6, Scirocco – chain jumps, valves bend); (2) EA189 2.0 TDI oil pump hex key wear (BKD, CBAB – sudden oil pressure loss, ruins crankshaft); (3) EA288 2.0 TDI AdBlue pump / NOx sensor failure (Passat B8, Tiguan – countdown to no-start); (4) 1.4 TSI Twincharger supercharger clutch & timing chain (Polo GTI, Golf Mk6); (5) EA188 PD 2.0 TDI crankshaft bearing & injector seal failure (BKP, AZV). Once these failures occur, full engine replacement is almost always the most cost‑effective solution.

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Volkswagen engine?
items.answer: Always compare multiple quotes – prices for the same engine code (e.g., BKD or CRLB) can vary by over £1,000 between suppliers. Consider used if you have a very tight budget, but reconditioned with upgraded components is better value for long‑term ownership. Ask for supply‑only if you have a trusted local garage – you save on the specialist’s fitting markup. Always check whether the quoted price includes delivery, fitting, VAT, and the old engine collection. Use our marketplace to see three or more quotes side‑by‑side, then choose the supplier that offers the best balance of price, warranty, and proximity.
